exemplar.title:
To what extent do governmental actions in Ecuador address women ' s menstrual
health?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent do governmental actions in Ecuador address women ' s menstrual
health?

strengths: Clear articulation of the research question, explicitly identifying Ecuadorian government actions on menstrual health within human rights, gender equality and public-health debates.
Personal justification effectively links field observations in Napo to structural inequality and course goals.
Explicit connection of core concepts (equality, power, human rights) to the case study demonstrates relevance to course content.
weaknesses: Personal motivation section is repetitive and could be more concise and focused.
Brief mention of gaps in existing literature lacks depth and needs expansion.
Minor phrasing lapses slightly reduce overall clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student clearly and explicitly identifies the political issue and provides a coherent rationale linking personal experience to broader course concepts, satisfying the descriptors for the 3â4 band. However, some repetition in the justification and only a brief discussion of literature gaps limit this to a 3 rather than a full 4.

strengths: Coherent description of both the survey and interview components, supported by annexes of questions and graphics.
Interview insights are effectively used to highlight structural obstacles and NGO roles.
Clear explanation of how engagement findings informed understanding of structural violence and policy gaps.
weaknesses: Omission of sample size, recruitment criteria and detailed sampling methodology reduces methodological credibility.
Organizational flow is uneven in places, affecting clarity.
Learning-connection discussion is somewhat descriptive and could be developed further.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The engagement is clearly described and relevant to the political issue, and the write-up articulates how experiential findings informed the studentâs understanding. Missing methodological details and uneven development of the learning connection constrain the score to 3 rather than 4.

strengths: Strong linkage between survey and interview data enriches the critical depth of the analysis.
Effective use of structural violence and SDGâ5 frameworks to ground key barriers in theory.
Contextualizing financial burdens against minimum wage robustly highlights economic inequality.
Multiâlevel analysis connects federal policy gaps to local barriers, adding depth.
weaknesses: Analysis lacks counterarguments and alternative policy perspectives for greater balance.
Core concept definitions remain generic and need deeper engagement with course literature.
Figures lack axis labels and clear narrative interpretation.
Some assertions (e.g., societal shame) require supporting citations or data.
Application of structural realism is introduced but not fully developed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates critical analysis beyond description, using theoretical lenses and multiâlevel evidence. Most main points are justified, and several strengths indicate movement toward the top of the 3â4 band. However, limited engagement with counterarguments and underdeveloped theoretical application preclude a full mark, placing it at 4.

strengths: Integration of NGO-led workshop insights with policy discussion shows synthesis of experience and theory.
Acknowledgement and comparison of multiple perspectives (government, NGOs, local women).
Structured, multi-level evaluation combining local education and national strategy proposals.
Balanced conclusion consistent with evidence, highlighting structural obstacles and investment needs.
weaknesses: Limited reflection on sample bias, ethical concerns and other methodological limitations.
Conclusions do not explore alternative policy scenarios or probe counter-arguments in depth.
Evaluation of power dynamics among actors remains superficial.
Theory and experiential insights remain only partially integrated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student acknowledges multiple perspectives, draws balanced conclusions and links experiences with theoretical frameworks, fitting the midâband descriptors. The synthesis remains partial, lacking deep theoretical integration and extensive evaluation of alternatives, limiting it to a 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student explicitly states the political issueâhow effectively Ecuadorian governmental actions address womenâs menstrual healthâand situates it within broader debates on human rights, gender equality and public-health policy. This explicit formulation satisfies the clarity requirement of the top band. Minor lapses in phrasing do not detract from overall clarity, so a score at the lower end (3) of the 3-4 band is appropriate.

The student provides a coherent description of both engagements: scope of the survey, sample diversity, key questions, and the focus of the interview. Annexes with questions and graphics further clarify what was done. Minor organisational issues and limited detail about sampling methodology prevent award of the full 4, so 3 is appropriate.

The write-up explains how survey findings on cost, stigma and health-system experience, together with interview insights, shaped the studentâs understanding of structural violence and policy gaps. Connections between experience and conceptual understanding are clear, though somewhat descriptive and unevenly developed, fitting a 3 in the top band.

Analysis moves beyond description by applying structural violence and structural-realist lenses, using survey statistics and VAT policy as evidence. However, exploration of competing explanations, data limitations and deeper theoretical application is limited, so the work reaches the mid-band (3-4) but not the highest. A 3 reflects moderate depth.

Equality, power and human rights are explicitly invoked and later used in analysis, demonstrating a clear link between the chosen engagement, the political issue and core course concepts. Because the discussion of theoretical content is present but not explored in great depth, a score of 3 within the top band is warranted.

Key claims (e.g., economic burden, rural exclusion, NGO role) are generally supported by survey percentages, price data and interview quotes, but several broader assertions about Latin America lack citation or evidence. Thus âsomeâ justification is achieved, meriting a 3 in the 3-4 band.

Perspectives of government officials, NGOs and women from varied socioeconomic backgrounds are acknowledged and compared, but the evaluation of each perspectiveâs validity or implications is modest. This meets âacknowledgedâ and partial evaluation, earning 3 in the 3-4 band.

The student links personal data with concepts of power and human rights and references structural realism, showing more than âlimited linksâ. Nonetheless the synthesis remains partial; theory is added to, rather than fully integrated with, experiential insights. Hence a mid-band score of 3 (within 3-4) is justified.

The student gives a clear personal justification, describing experiences in Napo where girls miss school and outlining a desire to raise awareness. The explanation links personal observation, structural inequality and course goals clearly, meeting the descriptors for the 3-4 band. The rationale, while clear, is relatively brief and somewhat repetitive; therefore a 3 rather than 4.

The conclusion restates findings, weighs NGO reliance against state responsibility, and aligns with evidence presented. While balanced, it does not probe counter-arguments or alternative outcomes in depth. The work thus fits solidly within the 3-4 band, meriting a 3.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly articulated, explicitly identifying governmental actions on menstrual health in Ecuador and situating the issue within human rights, gender equality and public health.
Personal motivation connecting observations in Napo to structural inequality is compelling but repetitive; consider refining to avoid redundancy and deepen the rationale.
Linking concepts of equality, power and human rights to the case study demonstrates a clear connection to core course content.
The brief mention of awareness-raising lacks depth; expand on why existing literature gaps in Ecuador motivated the engagement.
Begin the engagement description with details on survey sampling methodologyâhow were participants selected and how was diversity ensured?
The dual approach of survey and expert interview is well explained, offering complementary perspectives on individual experience versus policy analysis.
The survey description omits sample size and recruitment criteria; include these to strengthen the methodâs credibility and reproducibility.
The interview insights are used effectively to highlight structural obstacles and NGO roles, deepening the engagement narrative.
Strong linkage between interview and survey data shows how policy flaws surface in lived experiences, enhancing critical depth.
The three key issues are identified but lack critical engagement with counterarguments; incorporate alternative policy perspectives for depth.
Definitions of power, equality and human rights are generic; refine with course literature and apply them to Ecuadorâs context.
The figure presents key statistics but lacks axis labels and explicit interpretation in the narrative; add these to guide reader understanding.
Tying barriers to structural violence and SDG5 provides solid theoretical grounding for the political issue.
Assertion of societal shame would benefit from survey quantification or literature support to justify the claim.
Contextualizing financial burdens in relation to minimum wage effectively highlights economic inequality dimensions.
Highlighting the VAT removal under President Lasso is informative but needs precise citation and analysis of implementation impact.
The application of structural realism is introduced but remains descriptive; deepen the theoretical analysis by linking power dynamics to specific policy outcomes.
Multi-level analysis linking federal policy gaps to local-level barriers enriches the depth of issue exploration.
The portrayal of NGO reliance is descriptive; critically evaluate how NGO initiatives interact with state accountability for a richer synthesis.
Acknowledging NGO-led workshops highlights collaborative solutions, demonstrating integration of practical experiences with policy discussion.
Discussion of NGOâgovernment partnerships would benefit from linking these insights to theoretical frameworks on stateânonstate cooperation.
The proposal of a multi-level approach is well structured, combining local education and national policy strategies in a coherent evaluation.
Limitations section is concise but lacks reflection on sample bias and ethical concerns; expand this to solidify evaluation credibility.
The conclusion effectively underscores structural obstacles and investment needs, providing a balanced synthesis of findings.
Conclusions would be stronger by suggesting alternative policy scenarios and evaluating their potential outcomes.
The critique of NGO dependency raises accountability questions; consider deeper evaluation of power dynamics among actors for a richer conclusion.

rubricData.name: Global Politics IA: Engagement Activity
rubricData.description: For the new Global Politics syllabus

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue not identified or irrelevant
2. No meaningful explanation

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: No discussion of why the project matters

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities absent or unrelated to issue

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue named but explanation vague / superficial
2. Limited or missing context

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: Importance / suitability mentioned but weak or generic

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities listed; relevance implied or weakly linked

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue explicitly stated
2. Clear, precise explanation with relevant context

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: Importance / suitability clearly justified with specific reasons

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities fully explained and convincingly linked to the issue

strands.name: Integration of research â engagement
strands.content: No link between research and engagement

strands.name: Engagement planning
strands.content: Activities planned but timing / logistics patchy

strands.name: Integration of research â engagement
strands.content: Research consistently informs engagement; findings feed back into further inquiry

strands.name: Depth of political-issue analysis
strands.content: Clear, balanced analysis; key factors explored

strands.name: Synthesis of stakeholder perspectives
strands.content: Effective synthesis; perspectives woven into coherent argument

strands.name: Awareness of personal bias
strands.content: Biases explored; impact on work examined

strands.name: Organization & structure
strands.content: Logical structure; mostly easy to follow
